subject: First Aid & Safety
title: Urban Heat Islands
description: A tiny lesson with a big payoff: Urban Heat Islands.

hook.text: Downtown Phoenix is 10°F hotter than its outskirts. Concrete and asphalt store and radiate heat.

buildup.text: Urban heat islands form when buildings, roads, and lack of vegetation trap solar energy.

discovery.text: Nighttime temperatures in cities stay high because structures release stored heat after sunset.

twist.text: Poor neighborhoods have fewer trees and more asphalt—they experience more extreme heat island effects.

climax.text: Green roofs, reflective surfaces, and urban tree canopies can reduce urban temperatures by 5-10°F.

punchline.text: Cities bake themselves. Trees and white roofs cool whole neighborhoods.

quiz.question: What causes urban heat islands?
options: Buildings and roads absorb and radiate solar heat with too few trees to provide cooling
